mn44:0.2: The Shorter Series of Questions and Answers
mn44:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the Squirrels’ Feeding Place.
mn44:1.3: Then the lay follower Visākha went to the *bhikkhuni* Dhammadinnā. Having gone to the *bhikkhuni* Dhammadinnā, he paid homage to her and sat down to one side. Seated to one side, the lay follower Visākha said this to the *bhikkhuni* Dhammadinnā:
mn44:10.1: “But, madam, is the noble eightfold path *saṅkhata* or *asaṅkhata*?”
mn44:10.2: “The noble eightfold path, friend Visākha, is *saṅkhata*.”
mn44:11.1: “Now, madam, are the three aggregates included in the noble eightfold path, or is the noble eightfold path included in the three aggregates?”
mn44:11.2: “The three aggregates are not included in the noble eightfold path, friend Visākha; rather, the noble eightfold path is included in the three aggregates.
mn44:11.3: And, friend Visākha, right speech, right action, and right livelihood — these dhammas are included in the aggregate of ethical conduct.
mn44:11.4: And right effort, right *sati*, and right *samādhi* — these dhammas are included in the aggregate of *samādhi*.
mn44:11.5: And right view and right intention — these dhammas are included in the aggregate of discernment [*paññā*].”
mn44:12.1: “What, madam, is *samādhi*? What dhammas are the *nimittas* of *samādhi* [*samādhinimittā*]? What dhammas are the requisites of *samādhi* [*samādhiparikkhārā*]? What is the development of *samādhi* [*samādhibhāvanā*]?”
mn44:12.2: “Singleness of mind [*cittassa ekaggatā*], friend Visākha — this is *samādhi*.
mn44:12.4: The four right efforts are the requisites of *samādhi* [*samādhiparikkhārā*].
mn44:12.5: The cultivating, development [*bhāvanā*], and making much of those very dhammas — this is the development of *samādhi* [*samādhibhāvanā*] here.”
mn44:13.3: the bodily *saṅkhāra*, the verbal *saṅkhāra*, and the mental *saṅkhāra*.”
mn44:14.1: “What, madam, is the bodily *saṅkhāra*? What is the verbal *saṅkhāra*? What is the mental *saṅkhāra*?”
mn44:14.2: “In-breaths and out-breaths, friend Visākha, are the bodily *saṅkhāra*; thought and examination [*vitakkavicārā*] are the verbal *saṅkhāra*; *saññā* and *vedanā* are the mental *saṅkhāra*.”
mn44:15.1: “Why, madam, are in-breaths and out-breaths the bodily *saṅkhāra*? Why are thought and examination [*vitakkavicārā*] the verbal *saṅkhāra*? Why are *saññā* and *vedanā* the mental *saṅkhāra*?”
mn44:15.2: “In-breaths and out-breaths, friend Visākha, are bodily dhammas, bound up with the body. Therefore in-breaths and out-breaths are the bodily *saṅkhāra*.
mn44:15.3: First, friend Visākha, having thought [*vitakketvā*] and examined [*vicāretvā*], afterward one breaks out into speech. Therefore thought and examination [*vitakkavicārā*] are the verbal *saṅkhāra*.
mn44:15.4: And *saññā* and *vedanā* are mental dhammas, bound up with the mind [*citta*]. Therefore *saññā* and *vedanā* are the mental *saṅkhāra*.”
mn44:16.1: “How, madam, does the attainment of the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* come to be?”
mn44:16.2: “For a *bhikkhu* entering the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā*, friend Visākha, it does not occur:
mn44:16.3: ‘I will enter the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā*,’ or ‘I am entering the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā*,’ or ‘I have entered the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā*.’
mn44:16.4: Rather, his mind [*citta*] has previously been so developed [*bhāvitaṁ*] that it leads him to that state.”
mn44:17.1: “But for a *bhikkhu* entering the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā*, madam, which dhammas cease first: the bodily *saṅkhāra*, the verbal *saṅkhāra*, or the mental *saṅkhāra*?”
mn44:17.2: “For a *bhikkhu* entering the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā*, friend Visākha, the verbal *saṅkhāra* ceases first, then the bodily *saṅkhāra*, then the mental *saṅkhāra*.”
mn44:18.1: “How, madam, does emergence from the attainment of the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā* come to be?”
mn44:18.2: “For a *bhikkhu* emerging from the attainment of the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā*, friend Visākha, it does not occur:
mn44:18.3: ‘I will emerge from the attainment of the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā*,’ or ‘I am emerging from the attainment of the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā*,’ or ‘I have emerged from the attainment of the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā*.’
mn44:18.4: Rather, his mind [*citta*] has previously been so developed [*bhāvitaṁ*] that it leads him to that state.”
mn44:19.1: “But for a *bhikkhu* emerging from the attainment of the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā*, madam, which dhammas arise first: the bodily *saṅkhāra*, the verbal *saṅkhāra*, or the mental *saṅkhāra*?”
mn44:19.2: “For a *bhikkhu* emerging from the attainment of the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā*, friend Visākha, the mental *saṅkhāra* arises first, then the bodily *saṅkhāra*, then the verbal *saṅkhāra*.”
mn44:2.2: What, madam, is called identity by the *Bhagavā*?”
mn44:2.3: “These five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*upādānakkhandhā*], friend Visākha, are called identity by the *Bhagavā*,
mn44:2.4: that is: the *rūpa* aggregate of clinging/feeding [*rūpupādānakkhandho*], the *vedanā* aggregate of clinging/feeding [*vedanupādānakkhandho*], the *saññā* aggregate of clinging/feeding [*saññupādānakkhandho*], the *saṅkhāra* aggregate of clinging/feeding [*saṅkhārupādānakkhandho*], the consciousness [*viññāṇa*] aggregate of clinging/feeding [*viññāṇupādānakkhandho*].
mn44:2.5: These five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*pañcupādānakkhandhā*], friend Visākha, are called identity by the *Bhagavā*.”
mn44:2.6: “Sādhu, madam,” said the lay follower Visākha. Delighted with and approving what the *bhikkhuni* Dhammadinnā had said, he asked the *bhikkhuni* Dhammadinnā a further question:
mn44:20.1: “And when a *bhikkhu* has emerged from the attainment of the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā*, madam, how many contacts touch him?”
mn44:20.2: “When a *bhikkhu* has emerged from the attainment of the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā*, friend Visākha, three contacts touch him: contact of emptiness [*suññato phasso*], contact without *nimitta* [*animitto phasso*], contact without aim [*appaṇihito phasso*].”
mn44:21.1: “And for a *bhikkhu* who has emerged from the attainment of the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā*, madam, to what does his mind [*citta*] incline, to what does it slope, toward what does it tend?”
mn44:21.2: “For a *bhikkhu* who has emerged from the attainment of the cessation of *saññā* and *vedanā*, friend Visākha, his mind [*citta*] inclines to seclusion [*viveka*], slopes to seclusion [*viveka*], tends toward seclusion [*viveka*].”
mn44:23.1: “What, madam, is *sukha vedanā*? What is *dukkha vedanā*? What is neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha vedanā*?”
mn44:23.2: “Whatever bodily or mental *sukha*, agreeable felt experience —
mn44:23.4: Whatever bodily or mental *dukkha*, disagreeable felt experience —
mn44:23.6: Whatever bodily or mental felt experience that is neither agreeable nor disagreeable —
mn44:24.1: “But, madam, in what way is *sukha vedanā* *sukha*, and in what way *dukkha*? In what way is *dukkha vedanā* *sukha*, and in what way *dukkha*? In what way is neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha vedanā* *sukha*, and in what way *dukkha*?”
mn44:24.2: “*Sukha vedanā* is *sukha* in its continuance and *dukkha* in its change.
mn44:24.3: *Dukkha vedanā* is *dukkha* in its continuance and *sukha* in its change.
mn44:24.4: Neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha vedanā* is *sukha* in knowledge and *dukkha* in non-knowledge.”
mn44:25.1: “For *sukha vedanā*, madam, what underlying tendency [*anusaya*] lies behind it? For *dukkha vedanā*, what underlying tendency [*anusaya*] lies behind it? For neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha vedanā*, what underlying tendency [*anusaya*] lies behind it?”
mn44:25.2: “For *sukha vedanā*, friend Visākha, the underlying tendency to lust [*rāgānusayo*] lies behind it; for *dukkha vedanā*, the underlying tendency to aversion [*paṭighānusayo*] lies behind it; for neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha vedanā*, the underlying tendency to blindness [*avijjānusayo*] lies behind it.”
mn44:26.1: “Now, madam, does the underlying tendency to lust [*rāgānusayo*] lie behind every *sukha vedanā*? Does the underlying tendency to aversion [*paṭighānusayo*] lie behind every *dukkha vedanā*? Does the underlying tendency to blindness [*avijjānusayo*] lie behind every neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha vedanā*?”
mn44:26.2: “No, friend Visākha, the underlying tendency to lust [*rāgānusayo*] does not lie behind every *sukha vedanā*; the underlying tendency to aversion [*paṭighānusayo*] does not lie behind every *dukkha vedanā*; the underlying tendency to blindness [*avijjānusayo*] does not lie behind every neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha vedanā*.”
mn44:27.1: “For *sukha vedanā*, madam, what should be abandoned? For *dukkha vedanā*, what should be abandoned? For neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha vedanā*, what should be abandoned?”
mn44:27.2: “For *sukha vedanā*, friend Visākha, the underlying tendency to lust [*rāgānusayo*] should be abandoned; for *dukkha vedanā*, the underlying tendency to aversion [*paṭighānusayo*] should be abandoned; for neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha vedanā*, the underlying tendency to blindness [*avijjānusayo*] should be abandoned.”
mn44:28.1: “Now, madam, should the underlying tendency to lust [*rāgānusayo*] be abandoned in regard to every *sukha vedanā*? Should the underlying tendency to aversion [*paṭighānusayo*] be abandoned in regard to every *dukkha vedanā*? Should the underlying tendency to blindness [*avijjānusayo*] be abandoned in regard to every neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha vedanā*?”
mn44:28.10: By that he abandons blindness [*avijjā*], and the underlying tendency to blindness [*avijjānusayo*] does not lie behind that.”
mn44:28.2: “No, friend Visākha, the underlying tendency to lust [*rāgānusayo*] should not be abandoned in regard to every *sukha vedanā*; the underlying tendency to aversion [*paṭighānusayo*] should not be abandoned in regard to every *dukkha vedanā*; the underlying tendency to blindness [*avijjānusayo*] should not be abandoned in regard to every neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha vedanā*.
mn44:28.3: Here, friend Visākha, a *bhikkhu*, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful dhammas, enters and dwells in the first *jhāna*, with thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicāra*], born of seclusion [*viveka*], with *pīti* and *sukha*.
mn44:28.4: By that he abandons lust, and the underlying tendency to lust [*rāgānusayo*] does not lie behind that.
mn44:28.5: Here, friend Visākha, a *bhikkhu* reflects thus:
mn44:28.6: ‘Oh, when shall I enter and dwell in that dimension [*tadāyatanaṁ*] in which the noble ones now enter and dwell in that dimension [*āyatanaṁ*]?’
mn44:28.7: Thus, as he arouses longing for the unsurpassed liberations, dejection arises because longing is its condition.
mn44:28.8: By that he abandons aversion, and the underlying tendency to aversion [*paṭighānusayo*] does not lie behind that.
mn44:28.9: Here, friend Visākha, a *bhikkhu*, with the abandoning of *sukha* and the abandoning of *dukkha*, and with the earlier vanishing of joy and dejection, enters and dwells in the fourth *jhāna*, neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha*, with purity of equanimity and *sati*.
mn44:29.1: “For *sukha vedanā*, madam, what is the counterpart?”
mn44:29.10: “For knowledge, friend Visākha, liberation is the counterpart.”
mn44:29.11: “For liberation, madam, what is the counterpart?”
mn44:29.12: “For liberation, friend Visākha, *nibbāna* is the counterpart.”
mn44:29.14: “You have gone too far with the question, friend Visākha; you were not able to grasp the limit of questions.
mn44:29.15: For, friend Visākha, the holy life is immersed in *nibbāna*, has *nibbāna* as its destination, and has *nibbāna* as its final end.
mn44:29.16: And you, friend Visākha, should approach the *Bhagavā* and ask him about this matter. As the *Bhagavā* answers you, so you should remember it.”
mn44:29.2: “For *sukha vedanā*, friend Visākha, *dukkha vedanā* is the counterpart.”
mn44:29.3: “For *dukkha vedanā*, madam, what is the counterpart?”
mn44:29.4: “For *dukkha vedanā*, friend Visākha, *sukha vedanā* is the counterpart.”
mn44:29.5: “For neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha vedanā*, madam, what is the counterpart?”
mn44:29.6: “For neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha vedanā*, friend Visākha, blindness [*avijjā*] is the counterpart.”
mn44:29.7: “For blindness [*avijjā*], madam, what is the counterpart?”
mn44:29.8: “For blindness [*avijjā*], friend Visākha, knowledge is the counterpart.”
mn44:29.9: “For knowledge, madam, what is the counterpart?”
mn44:3.1: “‘The origin of identity, the origin of identity,’ madam, it is said.
mn44:3.2: What, madam, is called the origin of identity by the *Bhagavā*?”
mn44:3.3: “That *taṇhā*, friend Visākha, which leads to renewed becoming, accompanied by delight and lust, delighting here and there, that is:
mn44:3.4: *taṇhā* for sensual pleasures, *taṇhā* for *bhava*, *taṇhā* for non-*bhava*;
mn44:3.5: this, friend Visākha, is called the origin of identity by the *Bhagavā*.”
mn44:30.1: Then the lay follower Visākha, delighted with and approving what the *bhikkhuni* Dhammadinnā had said, rose from his seat, paid homage to the *bhikkhuni* Dhammadinnā, and keeping her on his right, went to the *Bhagavā*. Having gone to the *Bhagavā*, he paid homage to him and sat down to one side.
mn44:30.2: Seated to one side, the lay follower Visākha reported to the *Bhagavā* the whole of his conversation with the *bhikkhuni* Dhammadinnā.
mn44:30.3: When this was said, the *Bhagavā* said this to the lay follower Visākha:
mn44:30.4: “The *bhikkhuni* Dhammadinnā is wise, Visākha; the *bhikkhuni* Dhammadinnā is of great discernment [*mahāpaññā*], Visākha.
mn44:30.5: If you had asked me this matter too, Visākha, I would have answered it in exactly the same way as it was answered by the *bhikkhuni* Dhammadinnā.
mn44:30.6: That is exactly the meaning of it. And that is how you should remember it.”
mn44:30.8: Satisfied, the lay follower Visākha delighted in what the *Bhagavā* said.
mn44:30.9: The Shorter Series of Questions and Answers is finished, the fourth.
mn44:4.1: “‘The cessation of identity, the cessation of identity,’ madam, it is said.
mn44:4.2: What, madam, is called the cessation of identity by the *Bhagavā*?”
mn44:4.3: “The remainderless fading away and cessation of that very *taṇhā* — giving it up, relinquishing it, release, non-attachment:
mn44:4.4: this, friend Visākha, is called the cessation of identity by the *Bhagavā*.”
mn44:5.1: “‘The practice leading to the cessation of identity, the practice leading to the cessation of identity,’ madam, it is said.
mn44:5.2: What, madam, is called the practice leading to the cessation of identity by the *Bhagavā*?”
mn44:5.3: “This very noble eightfold path, friend Visākha, is called the practice leading to the cessation of identity by the *Bhagavā*, that is:
mn44:5.4: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right *sati*, right *samādhi*.”
mn44:6.1: “Now, madam, is clinging/feeding [*upādānaṁ*] the same thing as those five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*pañcupādānakkhandhā*], or is clinging/feeding [*upādānaṁ*] something apart from the five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*pañcahupādānakkhandhehi*]?”
mn44:6.2: “Clinging/feeding [*upādānaṁ*] is not the same thing as those five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*pañcupādānakkhandhā*], friend Visākha, nor is clinging/feeding [*upādānaṁ*] something apart from the five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*pañcahupādānakkhandhehi*].
mn44:6.3: Whatever desire-and-lust in relation to the five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*upādānakkhandhesu*], that is the clinging/feeding [*upādānaṁ*] there.”
mn44:7.1: “But how, madam, does identity view come to be?”
mn44:7.2: “Here, friend Visākha, an uninstructed ordinary person, who has not seen the noble ones, who is unskilled in the noble *dhamma*, undisciplined in the noble *dhamma*, who has not seen true persons, who is unskilled in the *dhamma* of true persons, undisciplined in the *dhamma* of true persons,
mn44:7.3: regards *rūpa* as self, or self as possessing *rūpa*, or *rūpa* as in self, or self as in *rūpa*.
mn44:7.7: He regards consciousness [*viññāṇa*] as self, or self as possessing consciousness [*viññāṇa*], or consciousness [*viññāṇa*] as in self, or self as in consciousness [*viññāṇa*].
mn44:7.8: That is how identity view comes to be, friend Visākha.”
mn44:8.1: “But how, madam, does identity view not come to be?”
mn44:8.2: “Here, friend Visākha, an instructed noble disciple, who has seen the noble ones, who is skilled in the noble *dhamma*, well disciplined in the noble *dhamma*, who has seen true persons, who is skilled in the *dhamma* of true persons, well disciplined in the *dhamma* of true persons,
mn44:8.3: does not regard *rūpa* as self, or self as possessing *rūpa*, or *rūpa* as in self, or self as in *rūpa*.
mn44:8.4: He does not regard *vedanā* as self …
mn44:8.7: he does not regard consciousness [*viññāṇa*] as self, or self as possessing consciousness [*viññāṇa*], or consciousness [*viññāṇa*] as in self, or self as in consciousness [*viññāṇa*].
mn44:8.8: That is how identity view does not come to be, friend Visākha.”
mn44:9.1: “And what, madam, is the noble eightfold path?”
mn44:9.2: “This very noble eightfold path, friend Visākha, that is:
mn44:9.3: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right *sati*, right *samādhi*.”
